title: Śrīcakrasaṃbara Tantra (Arthur Avalon / Kazi Dawa-Samdup)
subtitle: A Buddhist Yoginītantra of the Anuttarayoga class — the Heruka maṇḍala, five wisdoms, Vajra theory, and the union of Prajñā and Upāya

summary.one_line: The Śrīcakrasaṃbara Tantra (Tantrik Texts Vol. VII, ed. Arthur Avalon and Kazi Dawa-Samdup, 1919) — a complete Buddhist Yoginītantra with original Sanskrit, Tibetan, and English translation, coveri...
summary.scope: Covers the full translation of the root tantra (Parts I-VII), Avalon's Introduction on Vajrayāna and Mantrayāna, the five Dhyāni Buddhas and five wisdoms, the theory of Vajra as indestructible consciousness, the union of Prajñā (Wisdom/Śūnyatā) and Upāya (Method/Compassion), the Three Bodies (Dharmakāya/Sambhogakāya/Nirmāṇakāya), the Heruka maṇḍala with its 62 deities, mantra and bīja theory, transformations of the five skandhas into the five Buddhas, and the Clear Light (prabhāsvara) doctrine.

sources.label: Śrīcakrasaṃbara Tantra — Tantrik Texts Vol. VII, edited by Kazi Dawa-Samdup, with Introduction by Arthur Avalon (1919). Full PDF.

body.text: The term Vajra (rDo-rje) has as one of its meanings 'diamond', and so we get the quaint translation by some of 'Diamond Sow' for the Goddess Vajra-varahi (rDo-rje-Phagmo); and so also we find Vajrachitta translated as (though in the text it is explained as) 'diamond thought'. Vajra also means thunderbolt. The sense in which Vajra is used here is that of indestructibility; and its analogies are that as the diamond is the hardest of known substances, so Vajra is the indestructible; and as the thunderbolt is irresistible, so Vajra is that which is powerful and irresistible, so Vajra is used for That which is the ultimate indestructible, irresistible Reality. (Introduction, pp. 3-4)

Vajradhara (rDo-rje-hChang) embodies in Himself all the five Dhyani Buddhas and is called the sixth Buddha. He is the Dhyani Buddha of the thirteenth highest Vajra state or Bhumi in Sambhoga-kaya (bde-snang-rDorje-hDzin-pahi-sa). The five Dhyani Buddhas are Vajrasattva (East), Ratnasambhava (South), Amitabha (West), Amoghasiddhi (North), and Vairochana (Centre), and over them all is Vajradhara. He is of a blue colour and has two hands, the right hand holds a rDo-rje (Vajra) and the left a bell. (Introduction, pp. 21-22)

Vajra-sattva is represented sitting with the legs crossed, holding the Vajra (rDo-rje) in his right hand and in his left a Bell. The Bell is Shes-rab (Prajna) or Wisdom, which is Shunyata the Void, and the Vajra is Thabs (Upaya) or Method, which is Karuna or Compassion. This indicates that by means of the high and indestructible (Vajra) method, which is Compassion for all sentient beings, one attains the final end (Dharmakaya) through the Wisdom of the Void, and becomes the Embodiment of the 'Two in one' wisdom (Zung-hjug-yeshes), which is the Bhumi or state called Vajradhara (rDo-rje-hChang). (Introduction, pp. 23-24)

Shunyata is an inexpressible mystery in and behind the incessant flow of the stream of seeming, which can be described by no term borrowed from the world of appearance, for none of these terms has a meaning except in relation to its opposite. Thus 'one' is an idea belonging to the world of numbers; freedom and permanence are only understood with reference to the phenomena of dependence and change. The Advaita Buddhist Tantra is thus the doctrine of Shunyata. This means that the universe (hKhorva = Sangsara) and Nirvana are not two but one or Shunyata, just as the mind and body in any individual are aspects of one unity. (Introduction, p. 15)

The three bodies of Buddha are: (a) Dharmakaya (Chos-sku) — the pure, immaculate Dharma-body possessed of all the qualities of the highest truth; (b) Sambhogakaya (Longs-sku) — the body of bliss or enjoyment with which the Lord appears in the Akanishtha heaven for the benefit of Bodhisattvas; (c) Nirmanakaya (Sprul-sku) — the manifested body through which the Lord works for the salvation of all beings in the world. The Dharmakaya is the ultimate Void itself. (Introduction, pp. 27-28)

The Sanskrit term Sambhara is the equivalent of the Tibetan tshogs which means accumulation or collection. The term 'Chakra-Sambhara' means the accumulation of the circles (of Devatas) which compose the Mandala. It also has the meaning of 'vow' (samvara), and refers to the binding of the Ḍākinī-net (ḍākinījālasamvara), the process of union or mystical identification through creative visualization, whereby the adept achieves union with Śrī Heruka. (Introduction, p. 28)

The Perfect Mind (Byang-chub-sems) is the purified mind of the Buddha. The five aspects of the Buddha Consciousness are exoterically the five Dhyani Buddhas and esoterically the five Wisdoms (Yeshes). These are: (1) Dharmadhatu-Jnana — the wisdom which knows that the true nature of all things is Shunyata; (2) Adarsha-Jnana — the mirror-like wisdom; (3) Samata-Jnana — the equalising wisdom; (4) Pratyavekshana-Jnana — the distinguishing wisdom; (5) Kṛty-anusthana-Jnana — the wisdom which accomplishes all works. (Introduction, pp. 33-38)

The five skandhas (aggregates) are transformed into the five Dhyani Buddhas: the Rupa-skandha (form) becomes Vairochana; the Vedana-skandha (feeling) becomes Vajrasuryya; the Samjna-skandha (perception) becomes Padma-nateshvara; the Samskara-skandha (mental formations) becomes Raja-Vajra; the Vijnana-skandha (consciousness) becomes the revered and glorious Heruka. The practitioner meditates on himself as having become each a Tathagata. (Translation, Part I)

Then imagine that the Mantra Hūṃ itself gradually disappears, Ukara into the body of H or Hakara, the Hakara into the Nada, and the Nada into the Bindu, and then imagine the Bindu also disappears into the Void. This is followed by the meditation of the Clear Light (öd gsal). The nature of the Mind is Clear Light (Sems-kyi-rang-bZhin-hod-gSalvahi-ngo-vo) — the real clear light which is the nature of the Mind, the clear state of uncognizing consciousness. (Translation, Part I, notes)

In the philosophic presentment and its accompanying ritual, the idea of the Void (Shunyata) dominates. The worshipper first builds up the Devatas by the process of visualization (Creation Stage or bSkyed-rims), and then the Devatas, together with the worshipper himself, are absorbed into the Bindu (Thigle) between the eyebrows (Ajna-chakra) where is the Point of Light which itself disappears in the Void. (Introduction, pp. 5-6)

Heruka, otherwise called Lha-btsun-nam-mkhah-hiigs-med, is the Supreme Deity of this Tantra. He is the fierce form of the Buddha, standing on Bhairava and Kalaratri, the embodiment of Method (Upaya) — the Male Deity — while his consort Vajravarahi is the Voice of the Void (Shunyata) — the Female Deity as Wisdom (Prajna). They are ever in union (Yab-Yum), denoting that Wisdom and Method are never separate. The nine dramatic sentiments (nava-rasa) are present in His expression. (Introduction, pp. 8-12)

Imagine arising from Shunyata the Mantra Yaṃ, from that arises a wind Mandala (semicircular, green, with a banner), upon this the Mantra Raṃ, from that arises a fire Mandala (triangular, red), upon that a white Bindu (Thigle) from the Mantra Vaṃ, from that the water Mandala (circular, white), and from that a yellow Bindu from the Mantra Laṃ, from that the earth Mandala (square, yellow), upon that Mount Meru, and upon that the Mandala of the Devata arises. All these are considered to be of the nature of the Void and Vajra. (Translation, Part I, notes)

The Mandala of Shri-chakra-sambhara is comprised of four outer circles: first the Vajra-ground and Vajra-fence; then the circle of the eight burning grounds (śmaśāna); within that the circle of the eight great mountains; within that the circle of the clouds; and at the centre the celestial palace with four gates, each guarded by a door-keeper. In the centre is the lotus, within that the sun disc, and upon it Śrī Heruka embracing Vajravarahi, surrounded by the 62 deities of the Mandala arranged in three wheels: the circle of Ḍākinīs, the circle of Vajra-Ḍākinīs, and the circle of Khecarīs. (Translation, Part I)

The Mantrayanic Buddhist doctrine speaks of nine Yanas, each Yana being divided into four parts: the view (lTa-va; Skt. Drishti), the meditation (sGompa; Skt. Dhyana), the practice (sPyodpa; Skt. Charya), and the fruit (LBras-bu; Skt. Phala). The highest of these is Ati-yoga-tantra-yana, considered the pure Advaita-tantra or monistic school in which all is realised as the Eternal Buddha. The term Advaita (gNyis-med) means One, that is Shunyata. (Introduction, pp. 14-15)

In the XVIIth Chapter on Prajna-paramita of the Dvags-po it is said that Prajna separated from Method binds to Nirvana and Method separated from Wisdom binds to Sangsara. One should not rely on either (alone). It is the correct pursuit of both in union that leads to Liberation. This is the middle way of the Tantra: the union of the two truths, the inseparability of the Void and Compassion. (Translation, Part I, notes)

gaps: Full extraction of all 62 deities of the maṇḍala with their symbolism; complete mantra texts in Sanskrit
Detailed ritual procedures from Parts II-VII; the dance texts; the homa rituals

issues.title: Full ritual details from Parts II-VII not yet extracted
issues.description: The current RO covers the theory and foundational practices from the Introduction and Part I. The remaining parts contain detailed ritual procedures, dances, homa, and additional sādhanas.

issues.title: Comparative mapping to Tantraloka and daimon path not yet completed
issues.description: This text has direct parallels to the Tantraloka's Bhairava theology, the daimon path's Heruka encounter, and the clear light / bardo systems. These need to be extracted and mapped.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from the Śrīcakrasaṃbara Tantra (Avalon/Dawa-Samdup edition, 1919). Covers Vajra theory, five Buddhas and wisdoms, Prajñā-Upāya union, Śūnyatā, the Three Bodies, Heruka maṇḍala, mantra absorption into Clear Light, and skandha transformation. 15 passages extracted from the Introduction and Part I translation.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Śrīcakrasaṃbara Tantra PDF (9.9 MB, ~61,000 lines text). Avalon/Dawa-Samdup edition, Tantrik Texts Vol. VII.
